exercises.title: Classify as Command or Query
exercises.instructions:
Classify each as Command (write intent) or Query (read request):
A: PlaceOrderCommand(symbol='RELIANCE', qty=10)
B: GetPortfolioQuery(merchantId='MRC-001')
C: CancelOrderCommand(orderId='ORD-123')
D: GetTradeHistoryQuery(merchantId, from, to)
E: UpdateAccountBalanceCommand(merchantId, amount)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: COMMAND — PlaceOrderCommand
   Intent to change state: creates a new trade order
   Returns: OrderId only (not the full order state)
   Side effect: persists order + emits OrderPlacedEvent

B: QUERY — GetPortfolioQuery
   No side effects: reads current portfolio state
   Returns: PortfolioView (denormalized, pre-calculated)
   Source: read model (not write DB)

C: COMMAND — CancelOrderCommand
   Intent to change state: cancels an existing order
   Returns: void or OrderId
   Side effect: updates order status + emits OrderCancelledEvent

D: QUERY — GetTradeHistoryQuery
   No side effects: reads historical trade data
   Returns: List<TradeView> from read model
   Source: read model (optimized for historical queries)

E: COMMAND — UpdateAccountBalanceCommand
   Intent to change state: modifies account balance
   Returns: void or AccountId
   Side effect: persists balance change + emits BalanceUpdatedEvent

test_cases.expected:
A: COMMAND — PlaceOrderCommand
   Intent to change state: creates a new trade order
   Returns: OrderId only (not the full order state)
   Side effect: persists order + emits OrderPlacedEvent

B: QUERY — GetPortfolioQuery
   No side effects: reads current portfolio state
   Returns: PortfolioView (denormalized, pre-calculated)
   Source: read model (not write DB)

C: COMMAND — CancelOrderCommand
   Intent to change state: cancels an existing order
   Returns: void or OrderId
   Side effect: updates order status + emits OrderCancelledEvent

D: QUERY — GetTradeHistoryQuery
   No side effects: reads historical trade data
   Returns: List<TradeView> from read model
   Source: read model (optimized for historical queries)

E: COMMAND — UpdateAccountBalanceCommand
   Intent to change state: modifies account balance
   Returns: void or AccountId
   Side effect: persists balance change + emits BalanceUpdatedEvent

hints: Command: intent to change state, has side effects
Query: read-only, no side effects
Commands return IDs, Queries return data

exercises.title: Complete the CQRS command handler
exercises.instructions: Fill in the CQRS command handler structure.

blanks.hint: the specific command type this handler handles
commands return only the ID, not the full entity

hints: @Transactional: command handlers always transactional (write side)
Return ID only: not the full entity (that's a query's job)
eventBus.publish: emit event so projection can update read model

exercises.title: Command and Query record types
exercises.instructions:
Write for Zerodha:
1. PlaceOrderCommand record (merchantId, symbol, quantity, orderType)
2. GetPortfolioQuery record (merchantId)
3. PortfolioView record (merchantId, totalValue, positionCount)
4. Demonstrate: command → handler returns ID; query → handler returns view
5. @SpringBootTest: both types instantiate correctly
hints: record: immutable data types — perfect for commands and queries
Command handler returns ID (not entity)
Query handler returns View record (not JPA entity)

exercises.title: Portfolio projection — update read model on event
exercises.instructions:
Write PortfolioProjection:
1. @EventListener on OrderFilledEvent
2. @Async: don't block command handler thread
3. Updates PortfolioView in read store
4. @SpringBootTest: event received + view updated
hints: @EventListener: Spring event system (sync by default)
@Async: run projection in separate thread (non-blocking for command handler)
sleep(200): async projections need brief wait in tests — or use @Sync for tests

exercises.title: CQRS controller — separate command and query endpoints
exercises.instructions:
Write ZerodhaOrderController:
1. POST /api/orders: PlaceOrderCommand → returns 202 Accepted + orderId
2. GET /api/portfolio/{merchantId}: GetPortfolioQuery → returns PortfolioView
3. No entity returned from command endpoint
4. @WebMvcTest (2 tests): command returns 202, query returns view
hints: 202 Accepted: better than 200 OK for commands (acknowledged, not necessarily complete)
GET for query: idempotent, cacheable — correct HTTP semantics
@MockBean: mock command/query handlers in @WebMvcTest

exercises.title: Read model with denormalized portfolio view
exercises.instructions:
Design the PortfolioView table (denormalized for fast reads):
1. PortfolioView entity: merchantId, totalValue, positions (JSON), lastUpdated
2. Compare with normalized schema (many joins needed)
3. PortfolioViewRepository: findByMerchantId + updatePositions
4. @DataJpaTest: save + find portfolio view
hints: Denormalized: store pre-calculated values (no runtime joins)
positionsJson: serialize positions list as JSON string for speed
Read model entity: not the same as write model entity — different tables

exercises.title: Command validation before execution
exercises.instructions:
PlaceOrderCommand validation:
1. Validate: symbol not blank, quantity > 0 and <= 10000, orderType in {BUY,SELL}
2. If invalid: throw CommandValidationException (not IllegalArgumentException)
3. Validation happens in CommandHandler BEFORE DB write
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): valid passes, bad symbol fails, bad quantity fails
hints: CommandValidationException: domain-specific (not generic IllegalArgumentException)
Validate before any DB write: fail fast, avoid partial state
VALID_TYPES Set.of: immutable set for O(1) lookup

exercises.title: Multiple projections from one event
exercises.instructions:
OrderFilledEvent triggers 2 projections:
1. PortfolioProjection: updates merchant's portfolio total value
2. AnalyticsProjection: counts daily trade volume per symbol
3. Both are @EventListener + @Async (independent)
4. @SpringBootTest: one event → both projections update
hints: Multiple @EventListener on same event type: all fire independently
@Async: each projection runs in its own thread (parallel updates)
Independent projections: failure in one doesn't affect the other

exercises.title: CQRS with Axon Framework basics
exercises.instructions:
Show Axon Framework CQRS structure (most popular Java CQRS framework):
1. @Aggregate: ZerodhaOrder with @CommandHandler @EventSourcingHandler
2. @CommandHandler in aggregate: handle PlaceOrderCommand
3. @QueryHandler service: handle GetPortfolioQuery
4. Explain: why Axon vs manual CQRS
5. @SpringBootTest: aggregate command handler invokes correctly
hints: Axon @Aggregate: combines state + command handlers in one class
@CommandHandler: Axon wires handlers to command bus automatically
Event store: Axon persists every event (foundation for Event Sourcing in T2)

exercises.title: CQRS consistency analysis
exercises.instructions:
```
Timeline:
  09:00:00.000 — Merchant places trade: BUY 100 RELIANCE @ 2800
  09:00:00.005 — CommandHandler: order saved to write DB
  09:00:00.005 — OrderFilledEvent published
  09:00:00.010 — Merchant queries portfolio dashboard
  09:00:00.050 — PortfolioProjection processes event, updates read model
```

Q1: What does the portfolio dashboard show at 09:00:00.010?
Q2: Is this a bug?
Q3: What does it show at 09:00:00.100?
Q4: How do you communicate this to the end user?
exercises.expected_solution:
Q1: Portfolio at 09:00:00.010:
   Dashboard shows OLD portfolio value (before the BUY trade).
   The projection hasn't processed the event yet (takes until 09:00:00.050).
   40ms window where read model lags behind write model.

Q2: Is this a bug?
   NO — this is EXPECTED behavior in CQRS with eventual consistency.
   It's a design trade-off, not a defect.
   The system is 'eventually consistent': it will converge to the correct state.
   40ms lag is imperceptible to users in a portfolio dashboard context.

Q3: Portfolio at 09:00:00.100:
   Dashboard shows UPDATED portfolio value (after the BUY trade).
   Projection has processed the event by 09:00:00.050.
   At 09:00:00.100, read model is fully up-to-date.

Q4: Communicating to users:
   Option A: Show a 'refreshing...' indicator after trade placement
   Option B: Optimistic update in UI (show new value immediately, confirm later)
   Option C: Include 'last updated at 09:00:00.050' timestamp on portfolio view
   Option D: Document in API: 'portfolio view may lag up to 2 seconds after trade'
   Zerodha Kite: shows 'updating...' animation after order placement
   Don't use: 'synchronized' queries that wait for projection (defeats CQRS performance goal)

test_cases.expected:
Q1: Portfolio at 09:00:00.010:
   Dashboard shows OLD portfolio value (before the BUY trade).
   The projection hasn't processed the event yet (takes until 09:00:00.050).
   40ms window where read model lags behind write model.

Q2: Is this a bug?
   NO — this is EXPECTED behavior in CQRS with eventual consistency.
   It's a design trade-off, not a defect.
   The system is 'eventually consistent': it will converge to the correct state.
   40ms lag is imperceptible to users in a portfolio dashboard context.

Q3: Portfolio at 09:00:00.100:
   Dashboard shows UPDATED portfolio value (after the BUY trade).
   Projection has processed the event by 09:00:00.050.
   At 09:00:00.100, read model is fully up-to-date.

Q4: Communicating to users:
   Option A: Show a 'refreshing...' indicator after trade placement
   Option B: Optimistic update in UI (show new value immediately, confirm later)
   Option C: Include 'last updated at 09:00:00.050' timestamp on portfolio view
   Option D: Document in API: 'portfolio view may lag up to 2 seconds after trade'
   Zerodha Kite: shows 'updating...' animation after order placement
   Don't use: 'synchronized' queries that wait for projection (defeats CQRS performance goal)

hints: Eventual consistency: brief lag is expected, not a bug
40ms: imperceptible to humans for dashboard view
Document the behavior: tell clients the lag is expected

exercises.title: Full CQRS with write and read repositories
exercises.instructions:
Build complete CQRS trade system:
1. Order (write entity) + OrderRepository (write store)
2. TradeView (read entity, denormalized) + TradeViewRepository
3. OrderCommandHandler: save to write store + publish event
4. TradeProjection: @EventListener → update read store
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): command persists, projection updates
hints: Two separate tables: orders_write (normalized) and trade_view (denormalized)
@EventListener without @Async: synchronous projection (easier to test)
viewId: composite key (merchantId+symbol) for upsert-like behavior

exercises.title: CQRS trade history query with pagination
exercises.instructions:
Build a paginated trade history query:
1. TradeHistoryQuery: merchantId, page, size, fromDate, toDate
2. TradeHistoryView: list of trade summaries per merchant
3. TradeHistoryQueryHandler: reads from denormalized trade_view
4. @DataJpaTest: query returns correct paginated results
5. Repository: Page<TradeView> findByMerchantId(merchantId, Pageable)
hints: Pageable: PageRequest.of(page, size) — zero-indexed pages
OrderByExecutedAtDesc: most recent trades first (typical for history queries)
Read model entity: separate from write model even for history queries

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha CQRS system
exercises.instructions:
Build complete CQRS for Zerodha:
1. PlaceOrderCommand → OrderCommandHandler (validate + save + publish event)
2. OrderFilledEvent → PortfolioProjection (update read model)
3. GetPortfolioQuery → PortfolioQueryHandler (read from view)
4. REST Controller: POST /orders (202), GET /portfolio/{id} (200)
5. @SpringBootTest + @WebMvcTest (3 tests): command works, projection fires, query returns view
hints: Synchronous @EventListener: projection fires immediately (easier to test than async)
merge(key, value, BigDecimal::add): accumulate values in map
500 = 5 qty × 100 price (simplified calculation in test)

exercises.title: CQRS with Kafka event bus (production pattern)
exercises.instructions:
Design CQRS with Kafka as the event bus between write and read services:
1. OrderCommandHandler: publishes OrderFilledEvent to Kafka topic 'orders.filled'
2. PortfolioProjection: @KafkaListener consumes from 'orders.filled'
3. Why Kafka (vs Spring Events) for production microservices
4. @SpringBootTest: demonstrate the pattern with EmbeddedKafka or mock
hints: Kafka key=merchantId: ensures all orders from same merchant go to same partition (ordering)
@KafkaListener: Spring Kafka consumer — auto-commits offset after processing
Spring Events: same JVM. Kafka: cross-service (microservices CQRS)

exercises.title: Complete M24 T1 CQRS synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete M24 T1 synthesis:
1. PlaceOrderCommand + validation + event publishing
2. GetPortfolioQuery + handler (reads from projection)
3. PortfolioProjection: @EventListener updates view
4. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): valid cmd, invalid cmd throws, projection updates, query reads view
5. All 4 pass
hints: Synchronous @EventListener: projection fires synchronously in same test thread
price=BigDecimal(100): simplified price for test assertion math
merge(k,v,add): accumulate portfolio values across multiple orders
